thread.title: Justice Kumar bats for conflict resolution using psychology
thread.title_full: Justice Kumar bats for conflict resolution using psychology

author: Brendan Dabhi
title: Justice Kumar bats for conflict resolution using psychology
text:
skip to main content Sections English தமிழ் বাংলা മലയാളം ગુજરાતી हिंदी मराठी Business बिज़नेस Newsletters Edition India International Saturday, Nov 23, 2024 ePaper Today’s Paper Journalism of Courage Home ePaper My Express UPSC World India Explained Opinion Politics Entertainment Business Sports Cities Lifestyle Tech Research Videos Subscribe Sign In Trending Election Results LIVE Maharashtra Results Live Jharkhand Results Live Mini Crossword Premium 🎙️ Podcast Health & Wellness Advertisement News Cities Ahmedabad Justice Kumar bats for conflict resolution using psychology Justice Kumar bats for conflict resolution using psychology Highlighting the role of four Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) mechanisms in India and how one of them — conciliation — can be used by psychologists to end disputes at the police station level before they end up in courts, thereby increasing the burden on judicial system, Supreme Court Justice Aravind Kumar on Saturday said that […] Written by Brendan Dabhi Ahmedabad | November 24, 2024 04:41 IST 4 min read Justice Aravind Kumar
Highlighting the role of four Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) mechanisms in India and how one of them — conciliation — can be used by psychologists to end disputes at the police station level before they end up in courts, thereby increasing the burden on judicial system, Supreme Court Justice Aravind Kumar on Saturday said that this would be a great contribution to the nation.
Justice Aravind Kumar was speaking at the valedictory ceremony of the 2nd CPSICON 2024 National Annual Conference, themed “Strengthening National Security, Fostering Mental Health in the Digital Era.” The three-day conference, featuring over 600 delegates, was hosted by the School of Behavioural Sciences and Forensic Investigations (SBSFI) at Rashtriya Raksha University (RRU).
During the event, the judge said, “There is one particular section in the code of civil procedure, it is Section 89. Courts, lawyers, judges, police will step in when the conflict has taken place. But what is this conflict? It is a disagreement of ideas. I’ll give you a simple example. A sister demanding a partition of a father’s property from her brother. She will say look, even though I am married and staying with my husband my father has left so much property as a daughter I too am entitled under law (under Section 6c of the Hindu Succession Act)… I am entitled to be apportioned a part of the property. He (the brother) will say no you are educated, your marriage was performed… so much jewellery was given so that is equivalent to your share. Then the conflict starts.”
Advertisement He said, “From there the conflict goes to court. So all this emanated from conflict of ideas. This is where I say psychologists can step in. You ensure that there is no dispute, this conflict of ideas doesn’t blossom into a dispute. That is why there is Section 89 which says that the court must first make attempts at conflict resolution.”
Explaining conflict resolution, he said, “Which are the four methods? Arbitration, Mediation, Lok Adalat and Conciliation. While the first three are institutionally recognised, the fourth has not taken shape as institutional recognition. So Mr Bimal Patel (RRU Vice- Chancellor) and the clinical psychology society, I’m putting this as food for thought… The majority of such disputes which come to the police station, you can see that they are resolved. There will be failure ‘in’ attempt but there shouldn’t be failure ‘of’ attempt.”
He added, “If you try from now, it will get institutional recognition maybe 10 years, 20 years down the line, you can achieve this. So apart from achieving overall reduction in the pendency of cases, if we keep adding more cases, automatically the cases will build up. So if you start putting a bridge or rather a dam to see that such unwanted or frivolous litigation doesn’t get into the stream of litigation, the field of psychology will be contributing something great to the nation and this is what I hope and trust your institutions will do in this regard.”
During his closing remarks, Justice Aravind Kumar, talked about the use of Bharat being synonymous to India.
Advertisement He said, “…Though I have used the word India, if you see the Constitution preamble , the word is Bharat. That is why Mr Bimalbhai used the word Bharat. The Constitution says Bharat means India. You know, as to what name is to be kept, there was a committee constituted, it went throughout the length and breadth of the country. Ultimately, they said Bharat should be there. But Parliamentarians, in their wisdom, said they would put it as India. But they decided Bharat would be synonymous with India, a union of states. Bha-ra-ta, that is where we should be proud of being Indians. Bha is bhava, ra is raga and ta is tala. When all these three come together, it will be a good tune, a good song. And that is what we should strive to see that our country achieves excellence at all levels.”
